intro.value: Eq. 16 and Eq. 17 sit next to each other in the paper, but they do not answer the same question.
This reference explains the link: Eq. 16 finishes the asymptotic near-threshold story, while Eq. 17 starts a separate linear-stability analysis of incoherence.

content.value: Eq. 16 is a statement about asymptotic series. It tells you what it means to keep only the leading powers of a small parameter and ignore higher-order corrections.
That is why Eq. 14 can be interpreted as a near-threshold scaling law instead of an exact formula everywhere. Eq. 16 supplies the language that justifies that kind of approximation.

reference.value: Use Eq. 16 when the main question is: what is the leading behavior near a limit such as K going to K sub c?

content.value: Eq. 17 is not obtained by algebraically manipulating Eq. 16. It comes from a different construction: perturb the incoherent density, substitute that perturbation into the continuum equation, and keep only terms linear in the perturbation.
So the focus changes. Eq. 16 asks how to interpret an approximation near threshold. Eq. 17 asks whether a small perturbation of incoherence grows or decays in time.

reference.value: Use Eq. 17 when the main question is stability: if I perturb incoherence a little, does that disturbance die out or amplify?

sections.title: Why the Paper Places Them Consecutively

content.value: The equations are consecutive because they belong to the same broad synchronization story, not because one is a direct algebraic consequence of the other.

Eq. 14 to Eq. 16 complete the description of how the synchronized branch behaves near onset. Eq. 17 to Eq. 18 then address a separate issue the paper explicitly calls out: whether the incoherent state is stable at all.
So the link is conceptual: both live near the critical point, but one analyzes branch size and asymptotics, while the other analyzes stability through an eigenvalue problem.

reference.value: Eq. 16 tells you how to read a near-threshold approximation. Eq. 17 tells you how to test the linear stability of incoherence.
They are adjacent because the paper moves from existence and scaling of synchronized solutions to the separate question of stability.

content.value: If you are comparing exact and approximate formulas near K sub c, reach for Eq. 15 and Eq. 16.
If you are asking whether incoherence is linearly stable in the infinite-N noiseless limit, reach for Eq. 17 together with Eq. 18.

reference.value: Linear stability question for incoherence: Eq. 17 and Eq. 18.
